wp:category.wp:category_description: This is category description. You can edit or remove it.

wp:term.wp:term_description: This is category description. You can edit or remove it.

item.title: About
item.dc:creator: mike
item.content:encoded:
Mike is a demo blog for the Mike WordPress theme — a quiet place to show how posts, pages, and comments look in the wild. This About page stands in for the kind of short, personal introduction a real blogger might write.

There is not much to it: someone who likes minimalism, the occasional trip, and a stack of books that never gets shorter.

item.title: Contact
item.dc:creator: mike
item.content:encoded:
Want to get in touch? This is where a real site would drop an email address or a simple form. For the demo, imagine a friendly note inviting readers to say hello.

You could also follow along on your favourite social network — or not, which is also fine.

item.title: Privacy
item.dc:creator: mike
item.content:encoded:
What this site collects

This is a demo site for a WordPress theme. A real privacy policy would describe what data is collected, why, and how visitors can request its removal.

Comments left on posts store the name and email you provide, along with your comment. That is standard WordPress behaviour and can be edited or deleted by the site owner.

item.title: Page no title
item.dc:creator: mike
item.content:encoded:
This page has no page title. We made a checkbox in page editor to hide it.

Somewhere, a staircase practiced ascending itself, hoping one day to be promoted to a ladder. The other furniture found this ambitious but tactless. A lamp suggested therapy. The staircase, predictably, took offense and descended into a sulk lasting roughly four geometries.

The kettle considered its options before settling on a career in regional banking. Outside, the gravel rearranged itself into something resembling a polite suggestion, though no one had asked for one. A committee of clouds voted three to two against the idea of Tuesday, then adjourned to a nearby vending machine for further discussion.

item.title: Page with cover
item.dc:creator: mike
item.content:encoded:
Cover title. H1 here

Write anything here. Welcome to my page

Somewhere, a staircase practiced ascending itself, hoping one day to be promoted to a ladder. The other furniture found this ambitious but tactless. A lamp suggested therapy. The staircase, predictably, took offense and descended into a sulk lasting roughly four geometries.

The kettle considered its options before settling on a career in regional banking. Outside, the gravel rearranged itself into something resembling a polite suggestion, though no one had asked for one. A committee of clouds voted three to two against the idea of Tuesday, then adjourned to a nearby vending machine for further discussion.

item.title: Search
item.dc:creator: mike
item.content:encoded: This is dedicated search page. It's nothing but a normal page, I added "search" block via Gutenberg editor.

item.title: Static front page
item.dc:creator: mike
item.content:encoded:
Hi there! I'm Sarah

This is static front page. You can set this as static front page, to add welcome intro, then set another page as blog page to show your posts. The page title has been disabled, so above is custom <H1> tag.

Well, they’ve finally done it. Through raw force of will and more money than god, the nerds have finally done something that will “make the world a better place.” By that, I mean they’ve pooped out something will finally change the world in big ways, for better and worse.

As an early AI critic, I say this now, because the spicy autocomplete app finally excreted some text I could use, in the form of computer code that actually compiles. The AI hype prophesy made only five short years ago, that “it’ll be better tomorrow,” has finally come true.

My blog | About me | My projects | Contact

item.title: Accessibility
item.dc:creator: mike
item.content:encoded: Mike is the theme with a11y checked carefully.

item.title: The Coastal Towns Worth the Detour
item.dc:creator: mike
item.content:encoded:
Somewhere, a staircase practiced ascending itself, hoping one day to be promoted to a ladder. The other furniture found this ambitious but tactless. A lamp suggested therapy. The staircase, predictably, took offense and descended into a sulk lasting roughly four geometries.

The kettle considered its options before settling on a career in regional banking. Outside, the gravel rearranged itself into something resembling a polite suggestion, though no one had asked for one. A committee of clouds voted three to two against the idea of Tuesday, then adjourned to a nearby vending machine for further discussion.

Mathematics had taken the afternoon off, leaving its responsibilities to a confused parrot and a stack of unpaid receipts. The parrot, sensing opportunity, declared all even numbers to be merely a phase. Several spreadsheets quietly agreed but lacked the courage to say so aloud.

What I'd tell a friend

There was once a doorway that refused to be a doorway, preferring instead the quiet dignity of being a vague rumor.

A clock decided that punctuality was overrated and began telling time in flavors instead. Lunchtime now tasted faintly of confusion, while midnight insisted on being lemon. The townspeople adapted, scheduling their meetings around the inevitable aftertaste of half past purple.

People walked through it anyway, emerging slightly more skeptical of architecture. The hinges filed a complaint, which was promptly lost in a drawer of other complaints.

Nobody could explain why the soup kept insisting on being a verb. It conjugated itself at dinner parties and corrected the grammar of nearby vegetables. The carrots remained neutral, as carrots tend to do when the stakes are clearly imaginary.

Velocity sat down for a chat with the concept of beige, and the two of them agreed on absolutely nothing except the weather, which was technically not in attendance. A passing theorem offered snacks. Everyone declined, citing prior arrangements with a fictional Thursday.

The door way

The library had begun storing silence in jars, labeling each one with a date that had not yet been invented. Visitors browsed the shelves of quiet, occasionally borrowing a particularly loud emptiness for the weekend. Late fees were measured in regret and small pebbles.

The ocean mailed a postcard to a desert it had never met, describing in vivid terms a sandwich it had imagined the previous spring. The desert, touched, replied with a single grain of sand and a coupon. Neither party acknowledged the obvious logistical problems.

Quote author

Finally, the alphabet held a quiet rebellion, with the letter Q announcing it would no longer associate with U on principle. The vowels brokered a fragile peace involving cookies and a slightly suspicious treaty. By morning, everyone had forgotten the dispute, except the punctuation, which never forgets anything.

item.title: This post has wide images
item.dc:creator: mike
item.content:encoded:
Somewhere, a staircase practiced ascending itself, hoping one day to be promoted to a ladder. The other furniture found this ambitious but tactless. A lamp suggested therapy. The staircase, predictably, took offense and descended into a sulk lasting roughly four geometries.

The kettle considered its options before settling on a career in regional banking. Outside, the gravel rearranged itself into something resembling a polite suggestion, though no one had asked for one. A committee of clouds voted three to two against the idea of Tuesday, then adjourned to a nearby vending machine for further discussion.

Mathematics had taken the afternoon off, leaving its responsibilities to a confused parrot and a stack of unpaid receipts. The parrot, sensing opportunity, declared all even numbers to be merely a phase. Several spreadsheets quietly agreed but lacked the courage to say so aloud.

What I'd tell a friend

There was once a doorway that refused to be a doorway, preferring instead the quiet dignity of being a vague rumor.

A clock decided that punctuality was overrated and began telling time in flavors instead. Lunchtime now tasted faintly of confusion, while midnight insisted on being lemon. The townspeople adapted, scheduling their meetings around the inevitable aftertaste of half past purple.

People walked through it anyway, emerging slightly more skeptical of architecture. The hinges filed a complaint, which was promptly lost in a drawer of other complaints.

Nobody could explain why the soup kept insisting on being a verb. It conjugated itself at dinner parties and corrected the grammar of nearby vegetables. The carrots remained neutral, as carrots tend to do when the stakes are clearly imaginary.

Velocity sat down for a chat with the concept of beige, and the two of them agreed on absolutely nothing except the weather, which was technically not in attendance. A passing theorem offered snacks. Everyone declined, citing prior arrangements with a fictional Thursday.

The door way

The library had begun storing silence in jars, labeling each one with a date that had not yet been invented. Visitors browsed the shelves of quiet, occasionally borrowing a particularly loud emptiness for the weekend. Late fees were measured in regret and small pebbles.

The ocean mailed a postcard to a desert it had never met, describing in vivid terms a sandwich it had imagined the previous spring. The desert, touched, replied with a single grain of sand and a coupon. Neither party acknowledged the obvious logistical problems.

Quote author

Finally, the alphabet held a quiet rebellion, with the letter Q announcing it would no longer associate with U on principle. The vowels brokered a fragile peace involving cookies and a slightly suspicious treaty. By morning, everyone had forgotten the dispute, except the punctuation, which never forgets anything.

wp:comment.wp:comment_content: I packed way too much on my last trip too. Lesson learned.
Which notebook do you use? Always curious about other people's setups.
I disagree a little, but in a good way. Made me think.
Thanks for reading — really appreciate you saying so.

item.title: Three Days in a City I Couldn't Pronounce
item.dc:creator: mike
item.content:encoded:
Somewhere, a staircase practiced ascending itself, hoping one day to be promoted to a ladder. The other furniture found this ambitious but tactless. A lamp suggested therapy. The staircase, predictably, took offense and descended into a sulk lasting roughly four geometries.

The kettle considered its options before settling on a career in regional banking. Outside, the gravel rearranged itself into something resembling a polite suggestion, though no one had asked for one. A committee of clouds voted three to two against the idea of Tuesday, then adjourned to a nearby vending machine for further discussion.

Mathematics had taken the afternoon off, leaving its responsibilities to a confused parrot and a stack of unpaid receipts. The parrot, sensing opportunity, declared all even numbers to be merely a phase. Several spreadsheets quietly agreed but lacked the courage to say so aloud.

What I'd tell a friend

There was once a doorway that refused to be a doorway, preferring instead the quiet dignity of being a vague rumor.

A clock decided that punctuality was overrated and began telling time in flavors instead. Lunchtime now tasted faintly of confusion, while midnight insisted on being lemon. The townspeople adapted, scheduling their meetings around the inevitable aftertaste of half past purple.

People walked through it anyway, emerging slightly more skeptical of architecture. The hinges filed a complaint, which was promptly lost in a drawer of other complaints.

Nobody could explain why the soup kept insisting on being a verb. It conjugated itself at dinner parties and corrected the grammar of nearby vegetables. The carrots remained neutral, as carrots tend to do when the stakes are clearly imaginary.

Velocity sat down for a chat with the concept of beige, and the two of them agreed on absolutely nothing except the weather, which was technically not in attendance. A passing theorem offered snacks. Everyone declined, citing prior arrangements with a fictional Thursday.

The door way

The library had begun storing silence in jars, labeling each one with a date that had not yet been invented. Visitors browsed the shelves of quiet, occasionally borrowing a particularly loud emptiness for the weekend. Late fees were measured in regret and small pebbles.

The ocean mailed a postcard to a desert it had never met, describing in vivid terms a sandwich it had imagined the previous spring. The desert, touched, replied with a single grain of sand and a coupon. Neither party acknowledged the obvious logistical problems.

Quote author

Finally, the alphabet held a quiet rebellion, with the letter Q announcing it would no longer associate with U on principle. The vowels brokered a fragile peace involving cookies and a slightly suspicious treaty. By morning, everyone had forgotten the dispute, except the punctuation, which never forgets anything.

item.title: What I Actually Use From My Backpack
item.dc:creator: mike
item.content:encoded:
Somewhere, a staircase practiced ascending itself, hoping one day to be promoted to a ladder. The other furniture found this ambitious but tactless. A lamp suggested therapy. The staircase, predictably, took offense and descended into a sulk lasting roughly four geometries.

The kettle considered its options before settling on a career in regional banking. Outside, the gravel rearranged itself into something resembling a polite suggestion, though no one had asked for one. A committee of clouds voted three to two against the idea of Tuesday, then adjourned to a nearby vending machine for further discussion.

Mathematics had taken the afternoon off, leaving its responsibilities to a confused parrot and a stack of unpaid receipts. The parrot, sensing opportunity, declared all even numbers to be merely a phase. Several spreadsheets quietly agreed but lacked the courage to say so aloud.

What I'd tell a friend

There was once a doorway that refused to be a doorway, preferring instead the quiet dignity of being a vague rumor.

A clock decided that punctuality was overrated and began telling time in flavors instead. Lunchtime now tasted faintly of confusion, while midnight insisted on being lemon. The townspeople adapted, scheduling their meetings around the inevitable aftertaste of half past purple.

People walked through it anyway, emerging slightly more skeptical of architecture. The hinges filed a complaint, which was promptly lost in a drawer of other complaints.

Nobody could explain why the soup kept insisting on being a verb. It conjugated itself at dinner parties and corrected the grammar of nearby vegetables. The carrots remained neutral, as carrots tend to do when the stakes are clearly imaginary.

Velocity sat down for a chat with the concept of beige, and the two of them agreed on absolutely nothing except the weather, which was technically not in attendance. A passing theorem offered snacks. Everyone declined, citing prior arrangements with a fictional Thursday.

The door way

The library had begun storing silence in jars, labeling each one with a date that had not yet been invented. Visitors browsed the shelves of quiet, occasionally borrowing a particularly loud emptiness for the weekend. Late fees were measured in regret and small pebbles.

The ocean mailed a postcard to a desert it had never met, describing in vivid terms a sandwich it had imagined the previous spring. The desert, touched, replied with a single grain of sand and a coupon. Neither party acknowledged the obvious logistical problems.

Quote author

Finally, the alphabet held a quiet rebellion, with the letter Q announcing it would no longer associate with U on principle. The vowels brokered a fragile peace involving cookies and a slightly suspicious treaty. By morning, everyone had forgotten the dispute, except the punctuation, which never forgets anything.

wp:comment.wp:comment_content: Saving this to reread on a slow Sunday.
I packed way too much on my last trip too. Lesson learned.
This really resonated — I've been trying to do the same thing lately.
Thanks for reading — really appreciate you saying so.

item.title: This post uses Classic Editor
item.dc:creator: mike
item.content:encoded:
Somewhere, a staircase practiced ascending itself, hoping one day to be promoted to a ladder. The other furniture found this ambitious but tactless. A lamp suggested therapy. The staircase, predictably, took offense and descended into a sulk lasting roughly four geometries. The kettle considered its options before settling on a career in regional banking. Outside, the gravel rearranged itself into something resembling a polite suggestion, though no one had asked for one. A committee of clouds voted three to two against the idea of Tuesday, then adjourned to a nearby vending machine for further discussion. Mathematics had taken the afternoon off, leaving its responsibilities to a confused parrot and a stack of unpaid receipts. The parrot, sensing opportunity, declared all even numbers to be merely a phase. Several spreadsheets quietly agreed but lacked the courage to say so aloud.

What I'd tell a friend

There was once a doorway that refused to be a doorway, preferring instead the quiet dignity of being a vague rumor. A clock decided that punctuality was overrated and began telling time in flavors instead. Lunchtime now tasted faintly of confusion, while midnight insisted on being lemon. The townspeople adapted, scheduling their meetings around the inevitable aftertaste of half past purple. People walked through it anyway, emerging slightly more skeptical of architecture. The hinges filed a complaint, which was promptly lost in a drawer of other complaints. Nobody could explain why the soup kept insisting on being a verb. It conjugated itself at dinner parties and corrected the grammar of nearby vegetables. The carrots remained neutral, as carrots tend to do when the stakes are clearly imaginary. Velocity sat down for a chat with the concept of beige, and the two of them agreed on absolutely nothing except the weather, which was technically not in attendance. A passing theorem offered snacks. Everyone declined, citing prior arrangements with a fictional Thursday.

The door way

The library had begun storing silence in jars, labeling each one with a date that had not yet been invented. Visitors browsed the shelves of quiet, occasionally borrowing a particularly loud emptiness for the weekend. Late fees were measured in regret and small pebbles.
The ocean mailed a postcard to a desert it had never met, describing in vivid terms a sandwich it had imagined the previous spring. The desert, touched, replied with a single grain of sand and a coupon. Neither party acknowledged the obvious logistical problems. Quote author
Finally, the alphabet held a quiet rebellion, with the letter Q announcing it would no longer associate with U on principle. The vowels brokered a fragile peace involving cookies and a slightly suspicious treaty. By morning, everyone had forgotten the dispute, except the punctuation, which never forgets anything.

wp:comment.wp:comment_content: Simple and honest. That's rare.
Simple and honest. That's rare.
The blockquote in the middle is exactly it.
Good point, I might write a follow-up on that.

item.title: Notes on Note-Taking After Ten Years
item.dc:creator: mike
item.content:encoded:
Somewhere, a staircase practiced ascending itself, hoping one day to be promoted to a ladder. The other furniture found this ambitious but tactless. A lamp suggested therapy. The staircase, predictably, took offense and descended into a sulk lasting roughly four geometries.

The kettle considered its options before settling on a career in regional banking. Outside, the gravel rearranged itself into something resembling a polite suggestion, though no one had asked for one. A committee of clouds voted three to two against the idea of Tuesday, then adjourned to a nearby vending machine for further discussion.

Mathematics had taken the afternoon off, leaving its responsibilities to a confused parrot and a stack of unpaid receipts. The parrot, sensing opportunity, declared all even numbers to be merely a phase. Several spreadsheets quietly agreed but lacked the courage to say so aloud.

What I'd tell a friend

There was once a doorway that refused to be a doorway, preferring instead the quiet dignity of being a vague rumor.

A clock decided that punctuality was overrated and began telling time in flavors instead. Lunchtime now tasted faintly of confusion, while midnight insisted on being lemon. The townspeople adapted, scheduling their meetings around the inevitable aftertaste of half past purple.

People walked through it anyway, emerging slightly more skeptical of architecture. The hinges filed a complaint, which was promptly lost in a drawer of other complaints.

Nobody could explain why the soup kept insisting on being a verb. It conjugated itself at dinner parties and corrected the grammar of nearby vegetables. The carrots remained neutral, as carrots tend to do when the stakes are clearly imaginary.

Velocity sat down for a chat with the concept of beige, and the two of them agreed on absolutely nothing except the weather, which was technically not in attendance. A passing theorem offered snacks. Everyone declined, citing prior arrangements with a fictional Thursday.

The door way

The library had begun storing silence in jars, labeling each one with a date that had not yet been invented. Visitors browsed the shelves of quiet, occasionally borrowing a particularly loud emptiness for the weekend. Late fees were measured in regret and small pebbles.

The ocean mailed a postcard to a desert it had never met, describing in vivid terms a sandwich it had imagined the previous spring. The desert, touched, replied with a single grain of sand and a coupon. Neither party acknowledged the obvious logistical problems.

Quote author

Finally, the alphabet held a quiet rebellion, with the letter Q announcing it would no longer associate with U on principle. The vowels brokered a fragile peace involving cookies and a slightly suspicious treaty. By morning, everyone had forgotten the dispute, except the punctuation, which never forgets anything.

item.title: This post has no image
item.dc:creator: mike
item.content:encoded:
Somewhere, a staircase practiced ascending itself, hoping one day to be promoted to a ladder. The other furniture found this ambitious but tactless. A lamp suggested therapy. The staircase, predictably, took offense and descended into a sulk lasting roughly four geometries.

The kettle considered its options before settling on a career in regional banking. Outside, the gravel rearranged itself into something resembling a polite suggestion, though no one had asked for one. A committee of clouds voted three to two against the idea of Tuesday, then adjourned to a nearby vending machine for further discussion.

Mathematics had taken the afternoon off, leaving its responsibilities to a confused parrot and a stack of unpaid receipts. The parrot, sensing opportunity, declared all even numbers to be merely a phase. Several spreadsheets quietly agreed but lacked the courage to say so aloud.

What I'd tell a friend

There was once a doorway that refused to be a doorway, preferring instead the quiet dignity of being a vague rumor.

A clock decided that punctuality was overrated and began telling time in flavors instead. Lunchtime now tasted faintly of confusion, while midnight insisted on being lemon. The townspeople adapted, scheduling their meetings around the inevitable aftertaste of half past purple.

People walked through it anyway, emerging slightly more skeptical of architecture. The hinges filed a complaint, which was promptly lost in a drawer of other complaints.

Nobody could explain why the soup kept insisting on being a verb. It conjugated itself at dinner parties and corrected the grammar of nearby vegetables. The carrots remained neutral, as carrots tend to do when the stakes are clearly imaginary.

Velocity sat down for a chat with the concept of beige, and the two of them agreed on absolutely nothing except the weather, which was technically not in attendance. A passing theorem offered snacks. Everyone declined, citing prior arrangements with a fictional Thursday.

The door way

The library had begun storing silence in jars, labeling each one with a date that had not yet been invented. Visitors browsed the shelves of quiet, occasionally borrowing a particularly loud emptiness for the weekend. Late fees were measured in regret and small pebbles.

The ocean mailed a postcard to a desert it had never met, describing in vivid terms a sandwich it had imagined the previous spring. The desert, touched, replied with a single grain of sand and a coupon. Neither party acknowledged the obvious logistical problems.

Quote author

Finally, the alphabet held a quiet rebellion, with the letter Q announcing it would no longer associate with U on principle. The vowels brokered a fragile peace involving cookies and a slightly suspicious treaty. By morning, everyone had forgotten the dispute, except the punctuation, which never forgets anything.

wp:comment.wp:comment_content: I disagree a little, but in a good way. Made me think.
This really resonated — I've been trying to do the same thing lately.
I packed way too much on my last trip too. Lesson learned.
It's just a plain pocket notebook, nothing fancy.

item.title: Solo Travel and the Art of Being Bored
item.dc:creator: mike
item.content:encoded:
Somewhere, a staircase practiced ascending itself, hoping one day to be promoted to a ladder. The other furniture found this ambitious but tactless. A lamp suggested therapy. The staircase, predictably, took offense and descended into a sulk lasting roughly four geometries.

The kettle considered its options before settling on a career in regional banking. Outside, the gravel rearranged itself into something resembling a polite suggestion, though no one had asked for one. A committee of clouds voted three to two against the idea of Tuesday, then adjourned to a nearby vending machine for further discussion.

Mathematics had taken the afternoon off, leaving its responsibilities to a confused parrot and a stack of unpaid receipts. The parrot, sensing opportunity, declared all even numbers to be merely a phase. Several spreadsheets quietly agreed but lacked the courage to say so aloud.

What I'd tell a friend

There was once a doorway that refused to be a doorway, preferring instead the quiet dignity of being a vague rumor.

A clock decided that punctuality was overrated and began telling time in flavors instead. Lunchtime now tasted faintly of confusion, while midnight insisted on being lemon. The townspeople adapted, scheduling their meetings around the inevitable aftertaste of half past purple.

People walked through it anyway, emerging slightly more skeptical of architecture. The hinges filed a complaint, which was promptly lost in a drawer of other complaints.

Nobody could explain why the soup kept insisting on being a verb. It conjugated itself at dinner parties and corrected the grammar of nearby vegetables. The carrots remained neutral, as carrots tend to do when the stakes are clearly imaginary.

Velocity sat down for a chat with the concept of beige, and the two of them agreed on absolutely nothing except the weather, which was technically not in attendance. A passing theorem offered snacks. Everyone declined, citing prior arrangements with a fictional Thursday.

The door way

The library had begun storing silence in jars, labeling each one with a date that had not yet been invented. Visitors browsed the shelves of quiet, occasionally borrowing a particularly loud emptiness for the weekend. Late fees were measured in regret and small pebbles.

The ocean mailed a postcard to a desert it had never met, describing in vivid terms a sandwich it had imagined the previous spring. The desert, touched, replied with a single grain of sand and a coupon. Neither party acknowledged the obvious logistical problems.

Quote author

Finally, the alphabet held a quiet rebellion, with the letter Q announcing it would no longer associate with U on principle. The vowels brokered a fragile peace involving cookies and a slightly suspicious treaty. By morning, everyone had forgotten the dispute, except the punctuation, which never forgets anything.

item.title: Rereading the Same Five Books
item.dc:creator: mike
item.content:encoded:
Somewhere, a staircase practiced ascending itself, hoping one day to be promoted to a ladder. The other furniture found this ambitious but tactless. A lamp suggested therapy. The staircase, predictably, took offense and descended into a sulk lasting roughly four geometries.

The kettle considered its options before settling on a career in regional banking. Outside, the gravel rearranged itself into something resembling a polite suggestion, though no one had asked for one. A committee of clouds voted three to two against the idea of Tuesday, then adjourned to a nearby vending machine for further discussion.

Mathematics had taken the afternoon off, leaving its responsibilities to a confused parrot and a stack of unpaid receipts. The parrot, sensing opportunity, declared all even numbers to be merely a phase. Several spreadsheets quietly agreed but lacked the courage to say so aloud.

What I'd tell a friend

There was once a doorway that refused to be a doorway, preferring instead the quiet dignity of being a vague rumor.

A clock decided that punctuality was overrated and began telling time in flavors instead. Lunchtime now tasted faintly of confusion, while midnight insisted on being lemon. The townspeople adapted, scheduling their meetings around the inevitable aftertaste of half past purple.

People walked through it anyway, emerging slightly more skeptical of architecture. The hinges filed a complaint, which was promptly lost in a drawer of other complaints.

Nobody could explain why the soup kept insisting on being a verb. It conjugated itself at dinner parties and corrected the grammar of nearby vegetables. The carrots remained neutral, as carrots tend to do when the stakes are clearly imaginary.

Velocity sat down for a chat with the concept of beige, and the two of them agreed on absolutely nothing except the weather, which was technically not in attendance. A passing theorem offered snacks. Everyone declined, citing prior arrangements with a fictional Thursday.

The door way

The library had begun storing silence in jars, labeling each one with a date that had not yet been invented. Visitors browsed the shelves of quiet, occasionally borrowing a particularly loud emptiness for the weekend. Late fees were measured in regret and small pebbles.

The ocean mailed a postcard to a desert it had never met, describing in vivid terms a sandwich it had imagined the previous spring. The desert, touched, replied with a single grain of sand and a coupon. Neither party acknowledged the obvious logistical problems.

Quote author

Finally, the alphabet held a quiet rebellion, with the letter Q announcing it would no longer associate with U on principle. The vowels brokered a fragile peace involving cookies and a slightly suspicious treaty. By morning, everyone had forgotten the dispute, except the punctuation, which never forgets anything.

item.title: Coffee, and the Small Rituals That Hold a Day Together
item.dc:creator: mike
item.content:encoded:
Somewhere, a staircase practiced ascending itself, hoping one day to be promoted to a ladder. The other furniture found this ambitious but tactless. A lamp suggested therapy. The staircase, predictably, took offense and descended into a sulk lasting roughly four geometries.

The kettle considered its options before settling on a career in regional banking. Outside, the gravel rearranged itself into something resembling a polite suggestion, though no one had asked for one. A committee of clouds voted three to two against the idea of Tuesday, then adjourned to a nearby vending machine for further discussion.

Mathematics had taken the afternoon off, leaving its responsibilities to a confused parrot and a stack of unpaid receipts. The parrot, sensing opportunity, declared all even numbers to be merely a phase. Several spreadsheets quietly agreed but lacked the courage to say so aloud.

What I'd tell a friend

There was once a doorway that refused to be a doorway, preferring instead the quiet dignity of being a vague rumor.

A clock decided that punctuality was overrated and began telling time in flavors instead. Lunchtime now tasted faintly of confusion, while midnight insisted on being lemon. The townspeople adapted, scheduling their meetings around the inevitable aftertaste of half past purple.

People walked through it anyway, emerging slightly more skeptical of architecture. The hinges filed a complaint, which was promptly lost in a drawer of other complaints.

Nobody could explain why the soup kept insisting on being a verb. It conjugated itself at dinner parties and corrected the grammar of nearby vegetables. The carrots remained neutral, as carrots tend to do when the stakes are clearly imaginary.

Velocity sat down for a chat with the concept of beige, and the two of them agreed on absolutely nothing except the weather, which was technically not in attendance. A passing theorem offered snacks. Everyone declined, citing prior arrangements with a fictional Thursday.

The door way

The library had begun storing silence in jars, labeling each one with a date that had not yet been invented. Visitors browsed the shelves of quiet, occasionally borrowing a particularly loud emptiness for the weekend. Late fees were measured in regret and small pebbles.

The ocean mailed a postcard to a desert it had never met, describing in vivid terms a sandwich it had imagined the previous spring. The desert, touched, replied with a single grain of sand and a coupon. Neither party acknowledged the obvious logistical problems.

Quote author

Finally, the alphabet held a quiet rebellion, with the letter Q announcing it would no longer associate with U on principle. The vowels brokered a fragile peace involving cookies and a slightly suspicious treaty. By morning, everyone had forgotten the dispute, except the punctuation, which never forgets anything.

wp:comment.wp:comment_content: This really resonated — I've been trying to do the same thing lately.
Saving this to reread on a slow Sunday.
Lovely写, thank you. The part about defending time stuck with me.
Glad it landed. More coming soon.
